title: Mk.gee and the Guitar's New Language
author: Allastair Voss
description: Most guitar-forward albums today come in one of two flavors.
image_alt: Mk.gee and the Guitar's New Language
body:
## The Guitar Without the Guitar Album

[img:/assets/images/artists/mkgee-press-dazed.jpg]

Most guitar-forward albums today come in one of two flavors. There is the retro revivalist mode, where every choice gestures backward to something classic. Then there is the maximalist indie-adjacent mode, where the guitar gets layered into ambient textures until it becomes more of a mood than an instrument.

[spotify:album/6DlLdXBGCsSDPOV8R2pCl7]

Mk.gee's Two Star and the Dream Police is neither. It is one of those rare records where you cannot quite figure out what the guitar is doing or why it sounds that way, and that confusion is generative rather than frustrating.

## The Texture Question

The easiest comparison people reach for is Bill Frisell or early Robert Fripp, that school of guitar where the instrument gets deconstructed until it barely sounds like itself. But Mk.gee is not doing that either. The guitar on Two Star still sounds like a guitar. It has attack and sustain and all the recognizable physical qualities of a plucked string.

What changes is the context it gets placed in. The production creates this strange middle space where the guitar feels both intimate and distant at the same time. You can hear his fingers on the strings. You can hear the room. But the notes seem to arrive from somewhere just outside the room, like a transmission that has lost a little signal in transit.

[spotify:track/1jFmW64G9u4uNklySVI7BP]

That quality of slight displacement takes real craft to achieve. Making something sound effortful and effortless in equal measure requires the kind of calibration that only comes from knowing exactly what you are trying to achieve. The guitar work on tracks like Are You Looking Up feels practiced to the point of total ease, which is why it catches you off guard when a phrase turns slightly wrong, or stays on a note just a beat too long.

## Signal Chain as Composition

The specific sounds Mk.gee gets out of the guitar are worth isolating, because they do not come from conventional signal chains. There is a consistent use of very light overdrive sitting under what sounds like a clean amp, the kind of drive that does not announce itself but removes the harshest transients from the attack so that notes bloom rather than cut. Combined with the reverb choices, short and room-like rather than the lush plate reverb that indie rock defaults to, the guitar occupies a frequency space that keeps it from competing with the vocals while still holding its own weight in the arrangement.

The tremolo on certain tracks deserves specific attention. Tremolo is an effect that usually signals either vintage pastiche or psychedelia, and Mk.gee avoids both associations by running it at a rate that is slightly too fast to feel retro and slightly too irregular to feel mechanical. The result is a pulse that feels biological rather than electronic, like something breathing rather than oscillating.

Delay is used sparingly and precisely. There are no long ambient trails. When a delay appears, it is short enough to feel like an extension of the note rather than a separate event. That restraint keeps the guitar from spreading into spaces in the mix that belong to other elements.

## R&B Without the Safety Net

The album sits in an R&B adjacent space but refuses the genre's comfort zones. R&B in 2024 had settled into a certain production language: the trap hi-hats, the falsetto vulnerability, the confessional lyrics about relationships in various states of dissolution.

Mk.gee keeps some of that. The vulnerability is real. But the arrangement choices undercut the expected emotional payoffs. Where the genre template would give you a build and a release, Two Star often just holds the tension. It moves sideways when you expect it to move up.

That is a risky move. It can read as withholding. But on repeated listens it starts to feel like honesty. Like someone who knows what emotions you want them to perform and has decided to show you what it actually feels like instead.

The bass relationship with the guitar is central to how this works. In most R&B production, the bass carries rhythmic and harmonic information that the rest of the arrangement orbits. On Two Star, the bass is frequently passive, holding a note or a two-note movement while the guitar takes on the rhythmic function. The reversal creates a low-end that feels weightless compared to genre expectations, which gives the album a specific kind of emotional spaciousness. Things are not resolved at the bottom of the frequency spectrum. You have to find resolution somewhere else, or accept that it is not coming.

## What the Instrumentals Do

The instrumental passages on Two Star are where the album earns its reputation among guitar players. There is a clarity of intention that is rare. Every note is doing something. The choice to leave space rather than fill it, to let a phrase breathe, suggests someone who has listened to a lot of Bill Evans and understood that what you do not play is as important as what you do.

But this is not jazz guitar. The scales are wrong for that, or right in the wrong ways. There is more of a modal quality, like someone who grew up listening to early-80s Eno and then picked up a guitar and started asking what that sensibility would sound like with a fretboard.

The answer, on Two Star, is something that feels genuinely new. Not loudly new. Not new in a way that needs to explain itself. Just quietly different from what came before in ways that take time to locate.

## The Album as a Guitar Player's Object Lesson

Younger guitar players who are working out what to do with the instrument in a genre landscape that frequently has no obvious place for it should spend time with this record. The lesson it offers is not about technique in the conventional sense. Nobody is going to transcribe these parts and learn them as exercises. The lesson is about prioritization.

Mk.gee has clearly decided what the guitar is for on this record and organized every decision around that priority. It is not for showing off. It is not for filling space. It is there to carry a specific emotional frequency that the vocals and the production establish but cannot hold on their own. The guitar is load-bearing in a non-obvious way, which is the hardest kind of load-bearing to achieve.

The comparison that keeps arriving, despite not being an obvious one, is J Mascis at very low volume. Not in sound, but in the sense of a guitarist who has found an entirely idiosyncratic relationship to the instrument and committed to it so completely that the idiosyncrasy stops registering as odd and starts registering as correct. That is the standard Mk.gee is working at. On Two Star, he reaches it.
